coredata.dc:title: One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Rong, Mingcong
Feng, Yufeng
Wang, Yiru
Chen, Xi
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C dots) were prepared by an one-pot solid phase pyrolysis method using guanidinium chloride and citric acid as the catalyst, nitrogen and carbon source without any solvents. The synthesized N-C dots emitted intense blue fluorescence with high photostability and stability in high ionic strength. Under optimal conditions, determination for Fe3+could be completed in 10min with a wide linearity from 0 to 200μM and a detection limit of 100nM. The proposed method showed potential applications for Fe3+ detection in real samples. Moreover, the N-C dots were explored for cell imaging with excellent biocompatibility and stability, suggesting their great potential in bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-C dots
Solid phase pyrolysis
Fe3+
Fluorescence sensing
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the N-C dots
Fluorescence detection of the N-C dots towards Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The synthesis of fluorescent N-C dots. (B) Schematic illustration of the detection of Fe3+ using the N-C dots.
(A) TEM and HRTEM images of the N-C dots. Inset: the corresponding size distribution of the N-C dots. (B) FI-IR spectrum, (C) XPS spectrum, (D) C 1s spectrum and (E) N 1s spectrum of the N-C dots.

(A) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the N-C dots. Inset: photographs of the solution of the N-C dots under daylight (left) and 365nm UV light (right). (B) The emission spectra of the N-C dots under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 400nm. (C) Fluorescence responses of the N-C dots in different pH solutions (10mM borate buffer). (D) Stability of the N-C dots in the presence of different concentrations of NaCl.

(A) Fluorescence responses of the N-C dots in the presence of increasing concentrations of Fe3+ in 10mM borate buffer (pH 7.0). (B) The relationship between F0/F and the concentration of Fe3+. Inset: the linear responses to the concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 200μM. (C) Fluorescence responses of the N-C dots in 200μM various ions without and with 200μM Fe3+. F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of the N-C dots in the absence and presence of Fe3+ or other ions. (D) Time-resolved decay of the N-C dots with varying concentrations of Fe3+ in 10mM borate buffer (pH 7.0). Inset: lifetime value of the N-C dots with different concentrations of Fe3+.
Confocal micrographs of cultured cells. (A–C) MCF-7 breast cancer cells incubated with the N-C dots for 6h. (D–F) Control MCF-7 breast cancer cells.
Determination of Fe3+ spiked in real samples (n=3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: 1 is lake water, 2 is sea water, 3 is human plasma.

head.ce:title: One-pot solid phase pyrolysis synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Affiliated Hospital of Xiamen University
Department of Chemistry and the MOE Key Laboratory of Spectrochemical Analysis & Instrumentation, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361005, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C dots) were prepared by an one-pot solid phase pyrolysis method using guanidinium chloride and citric acid as the catalyst, nitrogen and carbon source without any solvents. The synthesized N-C dots emitted intense blue fluorescence with high photostability and stability in high ionic strength. Under optimal conditions, determination for Fe3+could be completed in 10min with a wide linearity from 0 to 200μM and a detection limit of 100nM. The proposed method showed potential applications for Fe3+ detection in real samples. Moreover, the N-C dots were explored for cell imaging with excellent biocompatibility and stability, suggesting their great potential in bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanomaterials, especially carbon dots, display excellent properties such as excellent optical properties, high photostability, good water-solubility, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, environmental friendliness and simple synthetic routes, have inspired extensive interest as potential competitors to conventional semiconductor quantum dots [1]. Since the discovery of carbon dots, a variety of methods have been found to synthetic carbon dots and regulate their physicochemical properties [2–7]. The most commonly used methods to tune their intrinsic properties are heteroatom doping, heteroatom doping can alter their intrinsic electronic characteristic and offer new active sites [8,9]. Nitrogen atom, possessing a comparable atomic size like that of the carbon atom, has been widely used for chemical doping of carbon dots [10,11]. Up to now, several methods have been developed for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-C dots), such as electrochemical, hydrothermal and microwave methods [12–14]. However, these approaches are limited by the low quantum yield (QY), low product yield or the use of organic solvent and dangerous catalyst. Thus, more facile synthetic methods to obtain high quality N-C dots are still urgently desired.

Ferric ion (Fe3+) is one of the most abundant elements on earth and a widely used industrial metal. As an essential trace element in a biological process, Fe3+ plays an important role in chelating with various regulatory proteins [10]. Therefore, determination of Fe3+ in the environmental monitoring and clinical research is of great significance. Plenty of N-C dots are reported sensitive to metal ions, such as Cu2+ [15], Hg2+ [2], Fe3+ [16]. More simple, rapid and highly sensitive methods for Fe3+detection using N-C dots are needed.

In this work, highly fluorescent N-C dots were prepared by an one-pot solid phase pyrolysis method using guanidinium chloride and citric acid as the catalyst, nitrogen and carbon source without any solvents. The proposed synthesis is free of acid, alkali, organic solvent, or further modification and passivation. The as-prepared N-C dots were 2.2nm in size and showed good water-solubility. They emitted intense blue fluorescence with a QY of 19.2%. And they exhibited photostability under high ionic strength and light illumination. Using the obtained N-C dots, an efficient sensing method for Fe3+ has been developed based on the static and dynamic fluorescence quenching, with a linearity ranging from 0 to 200μM and a limit detection of 100nM. Furthermore, the proposed approach showed potential application for Fe3+ determination in real samples and favorable biocompatibility in cell imaging.

Guanidinium chloride and citric acid were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (U.S.A.). All metal nitrates and chlorides of analytical grade were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Healthy human plasma samples were obtained from the First Affiliated Hospital of Xiamen University (Xiamen, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. All solutions were prepared using ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2MΩ/cm from a Millipore purification system (Millipore, U.S.A.).

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were obtained with a UV2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an F-4500 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan); Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 330 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron Corp., U.S.); and the fluorescence lifetime was performed on a FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (Horiba JobinYvon, France); transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected using a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 200kV; dynamic light scattering (DLS) and ζ-potential results were obtained from a Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, U.K.); X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were obtained on a PHI Quantum 2000 XPS system (Physical Electronics, U.S.) with Al Kα (hυ=1486.60eV); and cellular imaging was carried out using a Zeiss LSM 510 laser confocal scanning microscope (Zeiss, Germany).

In a typical synthesis procedure for N-C dots (Scheme 1 A), 1.125g guanidinium chloride and 0.375g citric acid were mixed in a 50mL round-bottom flask and heated at 225°C for 12min. After approximately 5min, the mixture solid turned into transparent liquid. Soon afterwards the liquid turned into light yellow, yellow and orange from 5 to 12min, corresponding to the pyrolysis and carbonization of the raw materials. The pyrolysis of citric acid accompanied with bubbles, the complete pyrolysis was finished at about 11min. Until the mixture cooled down, 5mL ultrapure water was added to dissolve the solid. Then the solution was transferred into a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000) and dialyzed overnight to obtain the pale yellow N-C dots solution. The productivity is about 20%, and the concentration of as-prepared N-C dots is 1mg/mL.

10μL stock solution of N-C dots was mixed with 10mM borate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing different concentrations (0, 0.1, 0.25, 0.75, 1.0, 2.5, 7.5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 200, 300, 400, 750 and 800μM) of Fe3+. The final volume of the solution was maintained at 1.0mL. After 10min of reaction at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra of the solution were collected in the wavelength from 360 to 600nm (the excitation wavelength was 340nm, both excitation and emission slits were 5nm).
In the investigation of the selectivity of Fe3+, several coexisting metal ions were selected and the concentration of all ions was 200μM. The same conditions were used as mentioned above.

In the investigation of Fe3+ in real samples, lake water samples were obtained in the Xiamen University campus and the sea water samples were collected from the sea around Xiamen University. Water samples were first centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 20min and then the supernatant was filtered through the 0.22μm water phase membrane. 10μL stock solution of N-C dots was injected into 10mM borate buffer (pH 7.0) solution containing 0.5mL water sample and different concentrations (1, 10 and 100μM) of Fe3+. The final volume of the solution was 1mL. The human plasma samples were used as received without further treatment, 20% human plasma was used in the detection.

Time-resolved decay measurements were tested to explore the reaction mechanism between the N-C dots and Fe3+. 10μL stock solution of N-C dots was mixed with 10mM borate buffer solution (pH 7.0) containing different concentrations (0, 10, 100μM and 1mM) of Fe3+. The final volume of the solution was 1mL. The excitation wavelength of the nanoled was 339nm and the emission wavelength was 425nm.

The vitro cytotoxicity of the N-C dots was evaluated using the standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-3,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) viability assay against normal human liver LO2 cells. Firstly, cells were seeded in 96-well plates with 1×104cells per well and cultivated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere overnight. The culture medium was discarded after cultivation for 24h. Then the cells were treated with 100μL of complete Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum (FBS) and different contents of N-C dots, and then incubated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for another 24h. The culture medium was removed and 20μL MTT (5mg/mL in 0.01M PBS, pH 7.4) was added into each well. After incubation for 4h, the MTT was washed three times with PBS, and then 100μL of DMSO was added into each well and shaken for 10min to fully dissolve the MTT. Finally, the optical density of each sample was recorded using a microplate reader (Multiskan FC, China) at a wavelength of 490nm. Cell viability was normalized based on that of control cells with PBS treatment.

The N-C dots were evaluated for bioimaging using their cell imaging in breast cancer MCF-7 cells. Cells were cultivated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Fluorescence confocal microscopy was used to observe the cell imaging of N-C dots. For microscopic observation, cells were seeded on cover slips in 24-well plates with 40,000 cells/cm2 per well in 0.5mL of DMEM containing 10% FBS and cultivated at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere for 24h. Then, the prior culture medium was discarded and 0.5mL of DMEM containing 10% FBS and 100μg/mL N-C dots were added into the well and cultivated for 6h at 37°C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Finally, the cells were washed three times with PBS and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde (in pH 7.4 PBS for 10min). The glass slides were sealed using one drop of fluoromount™ aqueous mounting medium and observed on laser confocal scanning microscope imaging system with an inverted laser confocal microscope. The control experiments involved the same procedure without the addition of N-C dots. Cellular fluorescence imaging was conducted at an excitation wavelength of 405nm.

Citric acid was selected as the carbon precursor because of its low cost, abundance and nucleating agent. Guanidinium chloride was used as the nitrogen precursor and the catalyst because of its nitrogen-rich characteristics. As shown in Scheme 1, citric acid and guanidinium chloride first proceeded dehydration and polymerization, and then carbonized into the fluorescent N-C dots.

The N-C dots were characterized by means of TEM, FT-IR and XPS. The typical TEM image of N-C dots showed a narrow diameter range from 1.4 to 3.3nm and an average size of 2.2nm (Fig. 1 A). The HRTEM image (upper right corner) showed an individual N-C dot with a lattice parameter of 0.210nm, which corresponded to the diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [17,18]. FI-IR and XPS measurements were further performed to explore the chemical structure of the N-C dots. The absorption bands at 1048, 1387 and 3429cm−1(Fig. 1B) were attributed to the stretching vibration of OH and NH [10,19]. The absorption bands at 1558 and 1650cm−1 corresponded to OCNH and CO, CN [20,21]. The absorption band at 2975cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of the CH [22]. As shown in Fig. 1C–E, the N-C dots contained C, N, O elements with a C/N/O ratio of 0.427/0.052/0.521, the nitrogen content is relatively high among the reported N-C dots [23–26]. The C 1s spectrum showed three peaks, centered at 284.8, 286.6 and 288.8eV, attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN and CN/CO bonds, respectively. The N 1s spectrum showed four peaks, centered at 398.5, 399.8, 401.0 and 403.2eV, corresponded to CN/CO, (C)3-N, CNH bonds and the charging effect [15,19,27]. All the results confirmed that the nitrogen atoms were doped into the structure of the N-C dots. The hydrated ionic radius of the N-C dots is about 4.5nm, suggesting the good monodispersity of the N-C dots in aqueous solution (Fig. S1). The carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on the N-C dots might give rise to their high water-solubility, corresponding with their zeta potential (−22.3mV in Fig. S2). In addition, there wasn’t any precipitation in N-C dots solution for one year at room temperature, which revealed their excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the N-C dots
ce:section.ce:para: In order to reveal the optical properties of the N-C dots, the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of the N-C dots were studied. The N-C dots showed an obvious absorption band at 340nm (Fig. 2 A), which derived from the major uniform size of the luminescence sp2 clusters in the N-C dots [22]. The maximum fluorescence excitation and emission wavelengths were 340 and 425nm. The intensive blue fluorescence of the N-C dots under 365nm UV lamp (inset photo in Fig. 2A) indicated their potential application in bioimaging. The fluorescence of the N-C dots was excitation dependent (Fig. 2B). Under excitation from 300 to 400nm, the corresponding fluorescence emission wavelength first kept at 425nm, then took a slight red-shift to 460nm. Using quinine sulfate as the reference, the QY of the N-C dots was estimated to be 19.2% (Table S1). The pH value had an obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of the N-C dots. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased at pH from 2 to 8, and remained almost constant from 8 to 10, which might be ascribed to their abundant negatively charged surface functional groups. As shown in Fig. 2D and S3, the N-C dots exhibited high photostability and resistance to high ionic strength, the fluorescence intensity of the N-C dots remained 92% of the original after continuous irradiation for 2h and showed constant fluorescence intensity in 1M NaCl solution. These results indicated the potential application of the N-C dots in fluorescence imaging under physiological conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of the N-C dots towards Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Most N-C dots are sensitive to metal ions because of their external abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups [2,28,29]. In the experiment, we explored the feasibility of the N-C dots for Fe3+ detection. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of the N-C dots led to a quick (10min) and obvious decrease with the addition of Fe3+. That demonstrated the potential of the prepared N-C dots for the determination of Fe3+. In order to gain the proper model method for the detection of real samples (water and human plasma samples), pH 7.0 was chosen for the following experiments. In the sensitivity study, the fluorescence responses of different concentrations of Fe3+ in the range of 0–800μM were investigated. The fluorescence intensity of the N-C dots gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, and showed a good linearity between the fluorescence intensity and the Fe3+ concentration from 0 to 200μM, with a detection limit of 100nM at a signal to noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 3 A and B). The results are comparable with previous reports for Fe3+ detection (Table S2). The fluorescence intensity versus the concentration of Fe3+ could be fitted into a linear equation of F0/F=0.0114[Fe3+] (μM)+0.972, with a correlation coefficient R of 0.993. The red-shift of the N-C dots emission peak upon the addition of high concentrations of Fe3+ to N-C dots can also be well-verified by the molecular interactions between Fe3+ and hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of the N-C dots [19,30].

To evaluate the selectivity of the N-C dots towards Fe3+ detection, the effects of the co-existing ions were investigated under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 3C, other ions showed ignorable interferences to the determination of Fe3+ within the linearity range. The application of the Fe3+ sensing approach was investigated in water and human plasma samples. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries varied in the range of 95–115%, suggesting the applicability of the proposed method to real samples.

To verify the detection mechanism of the N-C dots towards Fe3+ detection, the fluorescence lifetime of the N-C dots with varying concentrations of Fe3+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, the lifetime of N-C dots gradually decreased with the increase of Fe3+ concentration, which indicated that dynamic fluorescence quenching contributes to the N-C dots towards Fe3+ detection. At the same time, we tested the absorption spectra of the N-C dots in different concentrations of Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S5, there were two new absorption peaks at ∼300 and 333nm with the addition of Fe3+, implied that the static fluorescence quenching effect was another detection mechanism. These results are consistent with previous reports that hydroxyl, carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of carbon dots exhibited good binding affinity for Fe3+, and led to the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots [10,31,32]. Hence, the determination of Fe3+ was induced by dynamic and static fluorescence quenching.

The bright N-C dots are a potential material for cell imaging application. Cytotoxicity is an important factor for potential application in bioimaging. The biocompatibility of the N-C dots was evaluated by the viability of normal human liver LO2 cells after incubation with the N-C dots for 24h using a MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S6, cell viability remained constant even if the concentration of the N-C dots was as high as 500μg/mL, which indicated the excellent biocompatibility of the N-C dots. We estimated cell imaging of the N-C dots (100μg/mL) with MCF-7 breast cancer cells. As shown from the images carried on a confocal fluorescence microscopy with an excitation wavelength of 405nm (Fig. 4 ), the MCF-7 cells maintained normal cell activity and morphology. Bright blue fluorescence could be observed in the cells, mainly in the cytoplasm. These results demonstrated that the N-C dots were internalized by the MCF-7 cells and could be used as an efficient bioimaging material.

In this study, highly fluorescent N-C dots were synthesized by an one-pot solid phase pyrolysis method using guanidinium chloride and citric acid as the raw materials without any solvents. The N-C dots emitted strong blue fluorescence with high photostability and stability. Using the N-C dots as the fluorescence probe, an effective and facile fluorescence approach was developed for the determination of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. A wide linearity from 0 to 200μM and a detection limit of 100nM could be obtained for the detection of Fe3+. Furthermore, the determination mechanism between the N-C dots and Fe3+ was induced by both dynamic and static fluorescence quenching processes. The proposed method was applied in real samples for Fe3+ detection. Moreover, the N-C dots were explored for cell imaging with excellent biocompatibility and stability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Nature Scientific Foundation of China (No. 21375112), the Program for Changjiang Scholars and Innovative Research Team in University (No. IRT13036), the Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21521004), the Marine high-tech industry development projects of Fujian Province (No. 2015-19).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.02.014.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chem.-Eur. J.
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated graphene quantum dots
ACS Nano
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.

Anal. Chem.
High performance photoluminescent carbon dots for in vitro and in vivo bioimaging: effect of nitrogen doping ratios

Sens. Actuators B

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mingcong Rong received her Ph.D. degree in Xiamen University in 2016. She moved to Guangzhou University in 2016 as a lecture. Her research interests include synthesis of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials and their applications in bio/chemical sensing.

Xi Chen graduated from Fuzhou University with a Bachelor’s degree of analytical chemistry, and then obtained his Master’s degree there. Later in 1991, awarded the Monbusho Scholarship from Japanese government, he entered Kyoto Institute of Technology and received his Ph.D degree four years later. Between the year of 1996 and 1998, he performed as a postdoctoral researcher at Xiamen University, sooner after which, he was promoted as an associate professor at the department of chemistry. In 2003, he was promoted to be a full professor. He got the financial support from Japanese Society for the Promotion of Science (JSPS) to collaborate with Prof. M. Oyama, Kyoto University, in 2004 and 2009. Dr. Chen has taken charge of projects as National High Technical Development Project (863 Project) Foundation in the field of ocean, National Scientific Foundation of China and Key Project of Fujian Scientific Foundations, He was honored the Outstanding Contribution Award in 2002, and received the 1st class Scientific Progress Prize of Fujian province in 2005. His research interests cover electrochemical analysis using graphene materials, chemo/biosensors and pre-extraction materials applid in GC/HPLC.
